question: Which NestJS component is responsible for route-level authorization decisions?

options.text: Guard, because it allows or blocks execution using auth and role logic.
options.feedback: Correct: guards enforce access policy before controller handler execution.

options.text: Middleware, because it always has access to route metadata and roles.
options.feedback: Middleware runs earlier and is route-agnostic, so metadata access is limited.

options.text: Interceptor, because it only runs after responses are serialized.
options.feedback: Interceptors wrap execution pre/post handler; they are not primary authorization gates.

options.text: Pipe, because it transforms and validates request body authorization rules.
options.feedback: Pipes validate or transform data, not decide permissions for route access.

whyQuestion: Why use a guard instead of middleware for role checks?

whyOptions.text: Because guards integrate with execution context and route-specific metadata.
whyOptions.feedback: Exactly: guards can read decorators and apply per-handler authorization policies.

whyOptions.text: Because middleware executes after controllers and can inspect return values.
whyOptions.feedback: Middleware executes before routing, not after controller return processing.

whyOptions.text: Because interceptors cannot access request objects in NestJS.
whyOptions.feedback: Interceptors can access context, but authorization remains a guard concern.

whyOptions.text: Because pipes are disabled when `transform: true` is enabled.
whyOptions.feedback: Transform option belongs to validation pipe behavior and does not disable pipes.
